title: Doom Pilgrim - Random encounters of an otherworldly kind
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vQI78T3g4Gg

Daniel (@dungeondive) explores Doom Pilgrim, a deck-of-cards work of interactive fiction by Warclaw Games — rules printed right on the box, needing only the cards, a character sheet and a pencil. He's clear it's less a game than a work of weird fiction, and a strongly atmospheric one: he likens Warclaw's otherworldly art to the album covers of the Legendary Pink Dots and pitches it squarely at fans of Michael Cisco, Thomas Ligotti, Moorcock and FromSoft.

Mechanically it's deceptively simple, inspired by idle/clicker games: no dice or stat checks, just states you encounter. You draw three cards, banish one forever, bury one for later, and resolve the third — a tarot-like winnowing where the art guides your choice. Choices grant keywords, weapons, companions and badges, or inflict the bad things that end a run: lost paired limbs, three diseases, three curses, five wounds, or stacked "psychic drops" like depression and nihilism. Daniel narrates his own grim pilgrimage — knighted "Sir of the Dead Tree," cursed axe, rabies and all — through a split city and a venomously nihilistic hermit.

What excites him most is reuse: the cards' evocative scenes make superb encounter and NPC prompts for Mörk Borg, Runecairn, Sacrifice or any high-strangeness solo RPG.

----

Does a "barely a game, mostly a mood" deck like Doom Pilgrim appeal to you, or do you need real mechanical decisions to stay engaged?
